title: Worker 'error' event handler only logs to console, leaving the associated task permanently stuck with no user feedback.
description: The pool's worker-level `error` event (line 277: `w.addEventListener('error', (ev) => console.error('worker error:', ev))`) handles uncaught worker exceptions — for example, if the worker script itself throws at the module level or if the Worker process crashes. In this case, no 'error' message is posted through the normal protocol, so `_onMessage` never fires, the task is never deleted, the worker is never released, and the card stays in the 'busy' or 'encoding' state forever. The pool shrinks by one worker permanently.

title: WASM init failure surfaces as a caught error in the pipeline try/catch, but the wasmReady promise is cached in a rejected state and all subsequent calls will also fail.
description: `ensureWasm()` assigns `wasmReady = init()` once. If `init()` rejects (network failure fetching the .wasm, invalid module, etc.), `wasmReady` holds the rejected promise permanently. Every subsequent call to `ensureWasm()` awaits that same rejected promise, so every new file task dispatched to this worker will immediately throw, posting an 'error' back. The worker remains alive but useless. There is no retry logic and no signal to the main thread that this worker should be replaced.

title: reprocess_thumb_live silently swallows errors per taskId with an empty catch, providing no signal for systematic failures.
description: In the `reprocess_thumb_live` handler, errors from `applyLookToState` are caught with an empty `catch (err) {}`. If `apply_look` in WASM throws (e.g., buffer size mismatch, corrupted state), the gallery thumbnail silently stops updating. The main thread has no way to detect this condition. A degraded-mode indicator or at minimum a `console.error` would improve debuggability.

title: Object URL created for the JXL download link is never revoked when the card is re-processed, causing a memory leak.
description: In `onDone`, `URL.createObjectURL(blob)` creates a persistent object URL assigned to `link.href`. When `startConvert` is called again on the same card (re-process), the link's `href` is cleared (`link.removeAttribute('href')`) but `URL.revokeObjectURL` is never called on the old URL. Each re-process of the same file leaks a Blob-backed URL that holds the full JXL bytes in memory for the lifetime of the page.

title: JXL blob URL used for the inline download link has no revocation on lightbox close or page unload.
description: The lightbox download button (`lbDownloadBtn`) creates a Blob URL via `URL.createObjectURL` inside a `toBlob` callback and schedules `revokeObjectURL` after 30 seconds via `setTimeout`. This is a reasonable pattern, but `toBlob` is called on the lightbox canvas which is the RGB8 render, not the JXL. If the user clicks the download button many times in rapid succession, many short-lived Blob URLs accumulate until each 30s timer fires.

title: closeLightbox does not cancel in-flight liveInFlight state, so a pending reprocess_live response will still update the canvas after close.
description: When the lightbox is closed, `liveInFlight` is not reset to false and `livePendingLook` is not cleared. If a `reprocess_live` round-trip is in flight when the user closes the lightbox, the live handler will fire after close, set `liveInFlight = false`, and (if `livePendingLook` was set) trigger `triggerLiveUpdate` against `lightboxIndex = -1`, which `scheduleLiveUpdate` guards against. The canvas write is also guarded by `lightboxIndex >= 0`. So the visible effect is benign, but the unnecessary WASM compute still runs and the `liveInFlight` flag stays stuck as true until the response arrives.
